project_description.text: The Bedford-Pine Urban Renewal Area (Project Georgia A-2-1) is a 278.7-acre Neighborhood Development Program in eastern Atlanta, about 1.25 miles from City Hall, that has already produced a civic center, an elementary school, public housing, and hospital expansion since 1968. The remaining action is the disposition of a 78-acre Major Redevelopment Area to Park Central Communities, Inc. for roughly $11 million under a Comprehensive Development Plan. That plan calls for about 3,000 dwelling units (two-thirds owner-occupied), about 2.6 million square feet of office space, 250,000 square feet of retail, recreation facilities, and a coordinated rehabilitation of about 1,007 nearby multi-family units, to be built over roughly ten years.

affected_community.text: Bedford-Pine is an older inner-city Atlanta neighborhood that is predominantly Black and low-income, with median family income around $5,000, about 39% of residents below the poverty line, and a high share of elderly persons and renters in overcrowded, deteriorated housing. The community has higher-than-average crime rates, low school achievement scores at local elementary and middle schools, and heavy reliance on Grady Memorial Hospital for health care. Roughly 1,871 families were scheduled for relocation, and remaining residents include low-income households, a few homeowners, and small minority-owned businesses, represented by the active Bedford-Pine Project Area Committee (PAC).

alternatives_overview.text: The statement evaluates a do-nothing option, alternative sites, alternative land uses (predominantly commercial or predominantly residential), and several design alternatives, ultimately selecting Design Alternative C as the recommended action. It also considers variations in unit mix, density, and architectural prototypes, options for closing or relocating Felton Drive, an optional people-mover link to downtown, and a site-wide total energy system that was dropped for cost reasons. Additional alternatives address middle school capacity, relocation strategies (including on-site temporary housing and low-cost townhouses for remaining residents), refuse collection methods, and an institutional development approach using a Community Development Corporation, an SBIC, and a community foundation.

environmental_impact.text: Beneficial impacts include replacing slum and blighted conditions with new housing close to jobs and downtown, improved aesthetics, preservation of mature trees and topography, and stronger fire protection and employment opportunities. Adverse impacts include construction-period dust, noise, erosion, and traffic disruption; long-term increases in traffic, air pollution (with CO standards already exceeded in the area), and noise along Piedmont Avenue and the Stone Mountain Freeway corridor; about 1.58 million gallons per day of new sewage and large stormwater flows; and shortfalls in open space and middle-school capacity. Mitigation includes building setbacks, double glazing, landscape buffers and berms, on-site stormwater retention, erosion controls, traffic signal coordination, and expanded parks, though commitments of land, energy, water, and building materials are irreversible.

public_response.text: A lawsuit (Mildred West v. Atlanta Housing Authority) halted land conveyance until this EIS was completed, and a related case (Freeman v. Housing Authority) alleged NEPA non-compliance, while community pressure also produced a moratorium on displacement and a one-for-one replacement housing agreement. Many residents have expressed strong attachment to the neighborhood, reluctance to relocate, concern that reduced low-rent public housing breaks earlier promises, and frustration over inadequate shopping, services, and police response. The Bedford-Pine Project Area Committee, supported by an advocate planner, concurred in the Comprehensive Development Plan in February 1975 but also submitted detailed criticism that the draft EIS relied too heavily on the 1974 ECO-1 report, used outdated data, and underweighted citywide, perimeter, rehabilitation, and human impacts; federal and regional agencies (EPA, Interior, FPC, MARTA, ARC, DOT, GSA) raised additional concerns about energy analysis, cultural resources, air quality, transit assumptions, and uncoordinated road improvements.

value.name: Do-Nothing (No Action)
value.description: Termination of all project activities, leaving remaining relocation workload unresolved and vacant land to be developed without urban renewal plan controls, likely resulting in uncoordinated development.

value.name: Location of Other Sites
value.description: Consideration of alternative urban renewal sites elsewhere in Atlanta; rejected because the Bedford-Pine area had already been prioritized through local planning processes and was in active execution since 1968.

value.name: Predominantly Commercial Land Use
value.description: Development of the 78-acre site primarily as commercial/office space (approximately 7,800,000 sq ft) with only limited residential units; rejected as unmarketable within the build-out period and incompatible with community housing objectives.

value.name: Predominantly Residential Land Use
value.description: Development of nearly all parcels as residential with only a small commercial component in Parcel 17; rejected because it could not generate sufficient revenue to finance housing amenities or meet community goals for mixed-income development and job creation.

value.name: Design Alternative A
value.description: Commercial development arranged in a linear fashion along Piedmont Avenue north and south of the Civic Center, with housing occupying all areas to the east; rejected due to heavy traffic impacts on Piedmont, loss of attractive topography and trees, and forcing housing adjacent to the Stone Mountain Freeway.

value.name: Design Alternative B
value.description: Commercial development arranged linearly along both sides of Forrest Avenue with housing north and south of this commercial spine; rejected because it divided the housing into two poorly related halves and forced housing adjacent to the Stone Mountain Freeway and away from the community.

value.name: Design Alternative C (Proposed Action)
value.description: The selected mixed-use Comprehensive Development Plan integrating residential, commercial, and office uses across four sub-areas (Neighborhoods I, II, III, and the Commercial Core), chosen as the most feasible alternative with the best overall beneficial impacts.

value.name: Other Modifications
value.description: Program-mix adjustments including decreasing high-rise units in favor of low-rise high-density configurations, decreasing low-rise units to release land for open space, or increasing office/commercial space to generate additional surplus revenue while holding the residential program constant.

subthemes: Urban Expansion and Land Use Change

themes.justification: The project involves large-scale inner-city redevelopment with mixed-use housing and commercial construction (Urban Development subthemes), while the affected_community and public_response fields document significant displacement of low-income Black residents, litigation, and community activism (Place Based Development Conflicts). The public_response and alternatives_overview fields also highlight NEPA compliance disputes and multi-agency regulatory oversight (Governance and Institutional Control).

places.name: Bedford-Pine Urban Renewal Area, Atlanta, Georgia

location.note: 278.7-acre urban renewal area in eastern Atlanta, Fulton County, Georgia, bounded by Piedmont Avenue, North Avenue, Boulevard, and the Stone Mountain Freeway.
